nameMeanings.name: Wade
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: from the Middle English personal name Wade (Old English Wada or, via Norman French, from ancient Germanic Wado, of unclear origin but possibly related to Old English wadan ‘to go’).

English: topographic name from Middle English wade ‘ford’ (Old English (ge)wæd ‘ford, especially a tidal crossing’) for someone who lived by a ford, or a habitational name from a place so called, such as Wade Hall in North Cove (Suffolk).
Irish: shortened and altered from Mac Uaid, see McQuaid .

variants.name: Wadeson
meanings: English: patronymic from the Middle English personal name   Wade  .

variants.name: Wass
meanings: English (of Norman origin): from the Old French personal name Wace (ancient Germanic Waz(z)o), a pet form of any of various ancient Germanic personal names beginning with Wad- (compare Wade 1). The name Wace was common among the Normans, alongside the Parisian French variant Gace, and was latinized as Wazo (nominative), Wazonis (genitive).
English (of Norman origin): occasionally, perhaps a variant of Vause with vocalizing of initial V-.
German: nickname from Middle High German was(se) ‘sharp, cutting’.

variants.name: Waide
meanings: Irish: shortened form of Mac Uaid, see McQuaid .

variants.name: Waddy
meanings: English: variant of Wadey, perhaps from an unrecorded Middle English personal name Wady, a pet form of Wade , or in northern England alternatively a shortened form of Waldo . The name may also be a variant of Wilday .

variants.name: McQuaid
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Brendan, Colum, Nuala.
Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Uaid ‘son of Uad’, Gaelic form of the personal name Wat, a short form of Walter . Compare the Scottish form McQuate .

variants.name: Waithe
meanings: either a topographic name from Old Norse vath ‘ford’, or a habitational name from a place so named, such as Waithe (Lincolnshire) and Wath (North and Yorkshire).

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

historicEvents.title: State Lottery
historicEvents.description: A State Lottery was recorded in 1569. The tickets were sold at St. Paul's Cathedral in London.

historicEvents.title: The Royal Exchange is Organized
historicEvents.description: The Royal Exchange was organized in January 1571 by Sir Thomas Gresham. Gresham is known as the father of English banking.

historicEvents.title: The Honourable East India Company
historicEvents.description: The Honourable East India Company, a British joint-stock company, was established in 1600 for trade in the Indian Ocean region. At its height, the British East India Company had a private army which was twice the size of the British Army, ruled large sections of India, and revenues in the millions.
historicEvents.urlTitle: Historic UK - The East India Company

historicEvents.title: The Pilgrims Board the Mayflower
historicEvents.description: "The Pilgrims boarded the Mayflower in search of religious freedom in the New World in September 1620. The Mayflower set sail from Plymouth, England, with 102 passengers, 40 of whom called themselves ""Saints."" After two stormy months at sea, the ship reached the New World. Nearly half of the group died during their first winter due to malnutrition and the harsh New England winter."

historicEvents.title: The English Civil War
historicEvents.description: A series of conflicts regarding England's governance during the years 1642 to 1651 is now known as The English Civil War. Charles I summoned supporters to join him against his enemies in Parliament. In October 1642, nearly 10,000 men fought for Charles I and chased Parliament across the River Tamar. Fighting continued for years and was finally ended at the Battle of Worcester on September 3, 1651, with a Parliamentarian victory.

en.lifeSummary: When William Wade was born in 1562, in Halifax, Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom, his father, Henry Rice Wade, was 17 and his mother, Elizabeth Ramsden, was 29. He married Maria Preestlay on 20 December 1585, in Elland, Yorkshire, England. They were the parents of at least 1 son and 2 daughters.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque William Wade est né en 1562, à Halifax, Everwyck, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, son père, Henry Rice Wade, avait 17 ans et sa mère, Elizabeth Ramsden, avait 29 ans. Il a épousé Maria Preestlay le 20 décembre 1585, à Elland, Everwyck, Angleterre. Ils ont eu au moins 1 fils et 2 filles.

ko.lifeSummary: William Wade(이)가 1562년 영국 잉글랜드 요르크실르 할이파스에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Henry Rice Wade(은)는 17세였고, 그의 어머니 Elizabeth Ramsden(은)는 29세였다. 그는 1585년 12월 20일 영국 요르크실르 엘랜드에서 Maria Preestlay(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 한 명의 아들과 두 명의 딸을 두었다.
